topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The studio air felt thick, heavy enough to chew. Elias traced a line on the canvas with a trembling finger, the charcoal scratching against the surface sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet room. He paused, his breath catching in his throat. Near the edge of the depiction of the stormy sea, something was undeniably wrong. A minute detail, a pattern woven into the foam of a wave, looked too deliberate, too precise. He leaned closer, his knuckles whitening as he examined it under the harsh desk lamp. It wasn't a natural occurrence; it was a mark, a miniature tattoo rendered in shadow and texture, undeniably someone else's signature embedded within his creation. A cold prickle started at the base of his neck, and he quickly wiped his hand across his forehead, his gaze darting toward the studio door, half-expecting someone to materialize from the shadows.

[story 2]

Maya stared at the printout, her jaw tight enough to crack. The ink on the photograph of the portrait seemed to shift under the gallery lights. It was a swirling design, intricate and dark, placed precisely over the subject's eye. Her heart began to hammer against her ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. She picked up the original canvas, the linen cool beneath her fingertips, and brought it closer to the light. The detail resolved itself—a deliberate alteration, a permanent inscription added after she had finished the piece. A sharp intake of breath escaped her lips. She felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to flee, to slam the door and lock herself in. Every muscle in her body felt coiled, ready to spring, as if waiting for an unseen threat to manifest in the room.

[story 3]

"Impossible," whispered Julian, his voice barely audible above the distant city hum. He ran a hand repeatedly through his already disheveled hair, the movement jerky and agitated. He stepped back from the easel, his eyes fixed on the finished piece. There, in the deep indigo wash of the background, was the unmistakable signature of another artist—a complex, stylized script that mirrored the very aesthetic of his own work, yet felt alien. A wave of profound unease washed over him, chilling him to the bone despite the warmth of the studio. He swallowed hard, his mouth suddenly dry. He couldn't look away. He forced himself to breathe slowly, trying to anchor himself to the solid reality of the wooden floor, but the air felt thin, pressurized, as if something unseen was watching him through the glass.
